registry.description: Repentance is the turning — the change of direction in the inner person that results in a different way of living. The Hebrew vocabulary (shuv, nacham) covers returning, relenting, and the change of mind that leads to action; the Greek (metanoia) means a complete change of the inner orientation. Repentance is not guilt or sorrow alone, though both may accompany it; it is the actual turning from one direction and moving in another. God repents (changes his response) in the sense of relenting; human repentance is the return to God from a life turned away from him.

mti.status_note: ametathetos: unchangeable (occ=2, 2 verses) — Heb 6:17-18: 'the unchangeable character of his purpose... two unchangeable things in which it is impossible for God to lie'. Analytically significant as the negative form of the meta- (change) root applied to God: God is ametathetos — his purposes do not change. This provides the theological ground of hope for those who repent: the God to whom they return is unchangeable in his covenantal commitments. 2 verses.

verses.verse_text: Heb 6:17 So when God desired to show more convincingly to the heirs of the promise the unchangeable character of his purpose , he guaranteed it with an oath ,
Heb 6:18 so that by two unchangeable things , in which it is impossible for God to lie , we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us.

mti.status_note: metamorfoō: to transform/transfigure (occ=4, 4 verses) — transformation/transfiguration. Mat 17:2; Mar 9:2 — the Transfiguration; Rom 12:2 — 'be transformed (metamorfoō) by the renewal of your mind'; 2 Cor 3:18 — 'being transformed into the same image'. The transformation verb is analytically significant for repentance: the meta- prefix shared across the repentance family (metanoia, metamellomai) is here applied to the deepest possible change — the visible transformation of the person. Rom 12:2 is the repentance-transformation link: mind-renewal as the mechanism. 4 verses.

Rom 12:2 Do not be conformed to this world , but be transformed by the renewal of your mind , that by testing you may discern what is the will of God , what is good and acceptable and perfect .

2Cor 3:18 And we all , with unveiled face , beholding the glory of the Lord , are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another . For this comes from the Lord who is the Spirit .

mti.status_note: metatithēmi: to transfer/change/desert (occ=19, 5 verses) — to change position, transfer, desert. Gal 1:6 — 'so quickly deserting (metatithēmi) him who called you in the grace of Christ and turning to a different gospel'. Acts 7:16 — transported/carried. Heb 7:12 — change in the priesthood. The desertion sense (Gal 1:6) is the repentance contra-pattern: metanoia is turning toward God; metatithēmi (deserting) is turning away. 5 verses.

mti.status_note: me.shu.vah: faithlessness/apostasy/backsliding (occ=15, 12 verses) — the noun of apostasy derived from shuv. The structural opposite of repentance within the same root family. Pro 1:32 — the backsliding of fools destroys them; Jer 2:19, 3:6, 3:8, 3:11 — Israel's apostasy as the object of prophetic confrontation. This is the contra-term that defines repentance by negation: me.shu.vah is what repentance turns away from. 12 verses.

mti.status_note: shuv: rescue/restore sense (occ=124, 102 verses) — the restoration sense of shuv. God restoring Israel's fortunes, reversing exile, restoring health (Job 33:25-26). While not specifically 'repentance', the restoration sense is the divine-action counterpart to human repentance: when Israel turns back (H7725O/I), God restores (H7725H). The structural pairing of human turning and divine restoring is essential to the shuv theology. 102 verses.
